postTest.question: A 7-year-old with asthma is using accessory muscles, has intercostal and substernal retractions, and SpO2 of 91% on room air. Which of these findings most directly indicates the need for immediate oxygen?
options: A. Intercostal retractions
B. Accessory muscle use
C. SpO2 of 91%
D. Substernal retractions
postTest.rationale: SpO2 of 91% indicates significant hypoxemia (<94% is concerning, <90% is critical). While all findings indicate moderate-severe respiratory distress, the SpO2 of 91% is the direct quantitative measure of impaired oxygenation and is the clearest indication for oxygen supplementation. The other findings confirm distress but do not quantify hypoxemia.

distractorRationales.A: Intercostal retractions indicate moderate distress and increased work of breathing, but do not directly quantify oxygenation status.
distractorRationales.B: Accessory muscle use indicates significant respiratory effort but is a qualitative sign, not a direct oxygenation measure.
distractorRationales.D: Substernal retractions are similar to intercostal in significance; they do not quantify oxygen levels.

postTest.question: Head bobbing in a 4-month-old infant indicates the infant is using which muscle?
options: A. Diaphragm — the primary respiratory muscle is failing
B. Sternocleidomastoid — an accessory neck muscle recruited to open the airway
C. External intercostals — causing the head to move with each breath
D. Scalene muscles — causing forward head movement
postTest.rationale: Head bobbing in infants occurs when the sternocleidomastoid and other neck muscles are recruited as accessory respiratory muscles. Each contraction pulls the head forward and upward to open the airway, causing the characteristic bobbing motion. This indicates significant respiratory distress and impending fatigue.

distractorRationales.A: Diaphragmatic fatigue produces paradoxical breathing (abdominal inward movement during inspiration), not head bobbing.
distractorRationales.C: External intercostals contribute to chest wall expansion and do not produce head movement.
distractorRationales.D: Scalene muscles are accessory muscles of breathing but their contraction does not produce the characteristic head-forward-and-back bobbing pattern.

postTest.question: The nurse is assessing a 3-year-old post-tonsillectomy who has just developed a high-pitched musical sound heard clearly during both inspiration and expiration. This is most consistent with:
options: A. Polyphonic wheeze from reactive small airways disease
B. Biphasic stridor from fixed subglottic narrowing or edema post-procedure
C. Grunting indicating postoperative alveolar instability
D. Normal stridor from a relaxed pharynx during emergence from anesthesia
postTest.rationale: Biphasic stridor (present on both inspiration and expiration) indicates fixed narrowing at the subglottic level, which does not change with respiratory phase. Post-tonsillectomy subglottic edema or hematoma can cause this pattern. This is a serious sign requiring immediate assessment. Post-anesthesia stridor is possible but biphasic pattern warrants escalation.

distractorRationales.A: Polyphonic wheeze is a small airway finding — musical, multi-pitched, predominantly expiratory — not a biphasic high-pitched sound.
distractorRationales.C: Grunting is low-pitched, expiratory only, and heard in alveolar pathology — not a high-pitched musical biphasic sound.
distractorRationales.D: Post-anesthesia laryngospasm or stridor may occur but must be assessed and managed; it is not 'normal' and biphasic stridor requires escalation.

postTest.question: Which finding in a 14-month-old with bronchiolitis would most indicate transition from respiratory distress to respiratory failure?
options: A. Respiratory rate of 52/minute
B. Subcostal retractions present
C. SpO2 90% on room air
D. Decreased level of consciousness and reduced respiratory effort after appearing very distressed
postTest.rationale: Decreased level of consciousness (from anxious/distressed to lethargic) combined with decreased respiratory effort after a period of significant distress is the hallmark of transition from distress to failure. The child is no longer able to compensate — this represents impending respiratory arrest. Tachypnea, retractions, and mild hypoxemia are signs of distress, not failure.

distractorRationales.A: Tachypnea at 52/min is slightly elevated for a toddler (normal 20–30) and indicates distress; it is not a sign of failure.
distractorRationales.B: Subcostal retractions indicate mild respiratory distress — the most mild category of retractions.
distractorRationales.C: SpO2 of 90% indicates significant hypoxemia requiring intervention but is a distress finding; failure is indicated by mental status and effort changes.

postTest.question: Why is methemoglobinemia a cause of falsely low pulse oximetry readings?
options: A. MetHb absorbs light at wavelengths that cause the pulse oximeter to display approximately 85% regardless of actual saturation
B. MetHb prevents pulsatile blood flow, reducing signal quality
C. MetHb causes peripheral vasoconstriction that reduces signal detection
D. MetHb reads identically to COHb, causing falsely high readings
postTest.rationale: Methemoglobin absorbs the 660 nm and 940 nm wavelengths used by standard pulse oximetry in a ratio that causes the device to display approximately 85% regardless of actual saturation. This is a device-physics limitation. If true saturation is above 85%, the oximeter under-reads; if below 85%, it over-reads.

distractorRationales.B: MetHb does not directly prevent pulsatile flow; the error is in light absorption physics, not signal amplitude.
distractorRationales.C: Peripheral vasoconstriction can reduce signal quality but is not the mechanism of MetHb interference.
distractorRationales.D: COHb produces falsely HIGH readings (reads as OxyHb); MetHb produces a falsely fixed reading near 85% — these are different mechanisms.

postTest.question: A parent calls the clinic reporting their 2-month-old makes a crowing sound when inhaling and the sound is worse when the infant cries or is feeding but goes away when the infant is calm. What condition is most consistent with this description?
options: A. Croup (laryngotracheobronchitis)
B. Laryngomalacia
C. Epiglottitis
D. Subglottic stenosis
postTest.rationale: Laryngomalacia is the most common cause of chronic stridor in infants. The immature laryngeal cartilage collapses inward on inspiration, producing intermittent inspiratory stridor that worsens with increased respiratory demand (feeding, crying, agitation) and improves at rest. It typically presents in the first weeks of life and resolves by 12–18 months.

distractorRationales.A: Croup is a viral illness presenting acutely with barking cough and inspiratory stridor in toddlers 6 months–3 years; it would not come and go with activity level.
distractorRationales.C: Epiglottitis presents acutely with fever, drooling, muffled voice, and tripod positioning — not intermittent chronic stridor in an infant.
distractorRationales.D: Subglottic stenosis produces constant biphasic stridor that does not vary with crying or feeding.

postTest.question: During auscultation of a 5-year-old with pneumonia, the nurse hears fine crackles (rales) at the right lower lobe. What do fine crackles indicate?
options: A. Narrowed small airways with turbulent flow
B. Alveolar fluid, secretions, or atelectasis causing alveoli to pop open during inspiration
C. Turbulent airflow in the upper trachea
D. Pleural surfaces rubbing together
postTest.rationale: Fine crackles (rales) are generated when fluid-filled or collapsed alveoli pop open during inspiration. In pneumonia, inflammatory exudate in the alveoli and small airways causes the crackling sound. This is a localized finding consistent with consolidation.

distractorRationales.A: Narrowed small airways produce wheeze, not crackles.
distractorRationales.C: Upper tracheal turbulence produces stridor or rhonchi, not fine crackles.
distractorRationales.D: Pleural surfaces rubbing together produce a pleural friction rub — a leathery, creaking sound — not fine crackles.

postTest.question: A 5-week-old infant with RSV bronchiolitis has an SpO2 of 93% on 2L nasal prongs. The infant is feeding poorly and has subcostal retractions. Which intervention should the nurse prioritize?
options: A. Increase oxygen to 4L nasal prongs and reassess in 4 hours
B. Report findings to the RN including SpO2, feeding status, and retractions; ensure supplemental oxygen is running and IV access is available
C. Encourage the parent to attempt breastfeeding as this will improve respiratory mechanics
D. Apply a non-rebreather mask to maximize oxygen delivery
postTest.rationale: SpO2 of 93% despite supplemental oxygen, poor feeding, and visible retractions in a 5-week-old with bronchiolitis represents moderate respiratory distress requiring escalation. The RPN should report to the RN with complete assessment data, continue oxygen, and facilitate provider assessment. Simply increasing O2 without reporting is insufficient; applying a non-rebreather mask to an infant is not a first escalation step.

distractorRationales.A: Increasing O2 flow alone without reporting and reassessment is insufficient for a deteriorating infant; 4-hour reassessment is too long.
distractorRationales.C: An infant with poor feeding due to respiratory distress should not be pushed to feed — it increases work and risk of aspiration.
distractorRationales.D: Non-rebreather masks are not typically used as first-line in neonates and young infants; high-flow nasal cannula is more appropriate for escalation.

postTest.question: Which finding would make the nurse question the reliability of a pulse oximetry reading?
options: A. SpO2 of 98% on room air in a well-appearing child
B. A plethysmographic waveform that is flat or intermittent on the monitor
C. SpO2 of 92% in a child with confirmed pneumonia
D. SpO2 variation of 1–2% between fingers
postTest.rationale: The plethysmographic waveform (the pulse wave displayed on the oximeter) reflects the quality of the signal. A flat, intermittent, or dampened waveform indicates the sensor is not reliably detecting pulsatile flow — making the SpO2 number unreliable. Always assess waveform quality before trusting the numeric reading.

distractorRationales.A: A SpO2 of 98% in a well-appearing child on room air is expected and does not question reliability.
distractorRationales.C: SpO2 of 92% in a child with pneumonia is clinically expected and plausible — there is no reason to doubt reliability based on the number alone.
distractorRationales.D: Minor variation of 1–2% between fingers is expected and within device specification; it does not indicate unreliability.

postTest.question: In a child with severe upper airway obstruction (suspected foreign body), which position should the nurse encourage?
options: A. Supine with head of bed elevated 30 degrees
B. Left lateral decubitus to allow foreign body to shift
C. Sniffing or tripod position — position of comfort chosen by the child
D. Prone position to use gravity to shift the foreign body
postTest.rationale: A child with significant upper airway obstruction instinctively assumes the sniffing or tripod position to maximize airway diameter. The nurse must allow and support this position. Forced position changes, especially to supine, can precipitate complete obstruction. The child's self-selected position of comfort is the priority.

distractorRationales.A: Supine positioning (even elevated) reduces airway diameter compared to the sniffing position and can worsen upper airway obstruction.
distractorRationales.B: Lateral decubitus is not a standard position for upper airway foreign body; it may be used for specific lower airway foreign body physiotherapy but not for acute upper airway obstruction.
distractorRationales.D: Prone positioning for foreign body is not evidence-based for acute management and could worsen the situation.

flashcards.front: What is the retraction severity hierarchy from least to most severe?
flashcards.back: Subcostal (below last rib margin) = mild → Intercostal (between ribs) = moderate → Sternal (over sternum) = moderate-severe → Suprasternal (above sternum) = severe → Supraclavicular (above clavicle) = severe. Higher location = greater respiratory effort required.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: The compliant pediatric chest wall makes retractions visible with much less effort than in adults — retractions in a child always warrant assessment and documentation.
flashcards.memoryAnchor: Bottom to top = mild to severe. 'The higher the retractions, the harder the work.'

flashcards.front: What does grunting indicate in an infant and what is the mechanism?
flashcards.back: Grunting indicates alveolar instability — seen in pneumonia, RDS, pulmonary edema. Mechanism: the infant partially closes the glottis during early expiration to generate intrinsic PEEP, preventing alveolar collapse. Grunting is a sign of RESPIRATORY FAILURE, not merely distress. Requires immediate escalation.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Grunting is the infant's self-applied CPAP. When an infant is grunting, they are doing the work of a ventilator — and they will fatigue.
flashcards.memoryAnchor: Grunt = PEEP generation = alveolar instability = FAILURE. Not just distress — escalate now.

flashcards.front: What does inspiratory stridor versus biphasic stridor indicate about obstruction location?
flashcards.back: Inspiratory stridor = obstruction at the larynx or upper trachea (croup, epiglottitis, vocal cord foreign body). Expiratory stridor = lower trachea or mainstem bronchi. Biphasic stridor (both phases) = fixed subglottic obstruction that does not change with respiratory phase (subglottic stenosis, fixed foreign body, severe edema).
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Biphasic stridor is more concerning than purely inspiratory stridor because it indicates the narrowing is fixed and severe enough to cause obstruction in both respiratory phases.
flashcards.memoryAnchor: In = upper (above). Ex = lower (below). Both = fixed (stuck). Phase tells you the place.

flashcards.front: What are the normal respiratory rates for newborn, infant, toddler, school-age, and adolescent?
flashcards.back: Newborn (0–1 mo): 30–60/min. Infant (1–12 mo): 25–50/min. Toddler (1–3 yr): 20–30/min. Preschool (3–6 yr): 20–25/min. School-age (6–12 yr): 15–20/min. Adolescent (12–18 yr): 12–20/min. Count for full 60 seconds in infants due to irregular breathing.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Tachypnea is the most sensitive early sign of respiratory distress in children — know these numbers and recognize when the child exceeds the upper limit.
flashcards.memoryAnchor: Rates drop as kids grow: 60→50→30→25→20→20. Babies breathe fast; teens breathe like adults.

flashcards.front: Why is a silent chest in a child with asthma a pre-arrest emergency?
flashcards.back: A silent chest in asthma means air movement has ceased — the airways are so severely obstructed that no air is moving through them, eliminating the wheeze. This is NOT improvement; it is complete or near-complete obstruction. Without immediate intervention (bag-valve-mask, bronchodilator, emergency support), respiratory arrest follows.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: The wheeze disappears because there is nothing left to wheeze through — no air movement, no sound. Always compare to previous assessment; sudden quieting in an asthmatic child is an emergency.
flashcards.memoryAnchor: Wheeze disappears = air disappears = emergency. Silence in asthma is NOT golden.

flashcards.front: Why is standard pulse oximetry unreliable in carbon monoxide poisoning?
flashcards.back: Standard pulse oximetry uses two light wavelengths (660 nm and 940 nm). Carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) absorbs these wavelengths nearly identically to oxyhemoglobin (OxyHb). The device cannot distinguish them and displays a falsely normal or near-normal SpO2 reading while the patient may have 30–50% COHb. Clinical history and CO-oximetry are required for accurate assessment.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: In any smoke inhalation or CO exposure, apply 100% O2 via non-rebreather mask regardless of SpO2 reading. The SpO2 number is meaningless in CO poisoning.
flashcards.memoryAnchor: CO fools the machine — same light absorption as O2. SpO2 lies. Give O2 anyway.

flashcards.front: What is the tripod/sniffing position and what does it indicate clinically?
flashcards.back: Sniffing position: neck extended and chin forward, maximizing laryngeal and pharyngeal airway diameter. Tripod position: sitting forward with hands on knees or table, also expanding thoracic volume. These positions indicate significant upper airway obstruction. NEVER force a child in this position to lie down — supine positioning can precipitate complete obstruction.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: In suspected epiglottitis: drooling + tripod + fever + muffled voice = do NOT examine the throat, do NOT lay the child down, do NOT attempt IV until airway is secured.
flashcards.memoryAnchor: Tripod = 'I need every millimeter of airway I can get.' Respect the position.

flashcards.front: What distinguishes polyphonic wheeze from monophonic wheeze clinically?
flashcards.back: Polyphonic wheeze: multiple pitches simultaneously heard — many small airways narrowed at once → asthma, bronchiolitis. Monophonic wheeze: single constant pitch — one larger airway partially obstructed → foreign body aspiration, localized tumor, mucus plug. Monophonic wheeze that is new in a toddler warrants foreign body evaluation.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: A 2-year-old with new monophonic wheeze localized to one lung field who had a choking episode = foreign body aspiration until proven otherwise.
flashcards.memoryAnchor: Poly = many pipes, many pitches = asthma. Mono = one pipe stuck = foreign body.

flashcards.front: What are five clinical signs indicating respiratory failure rather than respiratory distress in a child?
flashcards.back: 1. Mental status change: from anxious/restless to lethargic or unresponsive. 2. Decreasing respiratory effort after a period of severe distress (fatigue). 3. Silent chest (absent air movement). 4. Cyanosis despite supplemental oxygen. 5. Apneic episodes. Any ONE of these findings signals respiratory failure requiring immediate escalation and preparation for airway support.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Distress = working hard to breathe. Failure = no longer able to work hard enough. The transition can be sudden — a child who was fighting may go quiet before arresting.
flashcards.memoryAnchor: MUDDLE: Mental status change, Underventilation, Disappearing breath sounds, Dusky/cyan, Lethargy, End-stage apnea.

flashcards.front: What plethysmographic finding makes a pulse oximetry SpO2 reading unreliable?
flashcards.back: A flat, intermittent, dampened, or absent plethysmographic waveform indicates the pulse oximeter is not detecting reliable pulsatile blood flow. Causes: poor perfusion, cold extremities, motion, vasoconstriction, tight sensor. Always assess waveform quality BEFORE trusting the SpO2 number. A poor-quality waveform = unreliable SpO2.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Many pulse oximeters display the pulse waveform on screen — a clean, tall, regular wave confirms reliable reading. If the wave is flat or choppy, the number cannot be trusted.
flashcards.memoryAnchor: Good wave = good number. Bad wave = bad data. Check the waveform, not just the number.

imagePlaceholders.alt: Diagram of pediatric chest showing retraction locations by anatomical site with severity labels
imagePlaceholders.caption: Retraction Locations in Pediatric Respiratory Distress: Anatomical sites with corresponding severity level from subcostal (mild) to supraclavicular (severe).
imagePlaceholders.suggestedContent: Anterior view of infant chest with labeled arrows pointing to: subcostal (below rib margin, labeled mild), intercostal (between ribs, labeled moderate), sternal (over sternum, labeled moderate-severe), suprasternal (above sternum/jugular notch, labeled severe), supraclavicular (above clavicle, labeled severe). Include color gradient from yellow to red indicating increasing severity.

imagePlaceholders.alt: Comparison diagram of stridor types by airway location with breathing phase indicators
imagePlaceholders.caption: Stridor Type by Obstruction Location: Inspiratory, expiratory, and biphasic stridor correspond to distinct anatomical sites of airway narrowing.
imagePlaceholders.suggestedContent: Sagittal cross-section of upper airway from nasopharynx to bronchi with three labeled zones: Zone 1 (larynx/upper trachea) = Inspiratory stridor with downward arrow; Zone 2 (lower trachea) = Expiratory stridor with upward arrow; Zone 3 (fixed subglottic) = Biphasic stridor with both arrows. Include representative clinical examples (croup, foreign body, stenosis).

documentationConsiderations: Document respiratory rate as a counted value over 60 seconds, not estimated; include the specific number alongside the age-appropriate normal range.

Describe retractions by anatomical location (subcostal, intercostal, sternal, suprasternal, supraclavicular) rather than 'mild/moderate/severe' alone, as anatomical location is objective and verifiable.
Document SpO2 with oxygen delivery method and flow rate (e.g., 'SpO2 93% on 2L nasal prongs') and note waveform quality (good/poor signal); an SpO2 reading without context is clinically incomplete.
If a child is adopting a position of comfort (tripod, sniffing), document this explicitly — it is a diagnostic physical finding indicating significant airway obstruction.
references: Ralston SL, Lieberthal AS, Meissner HC, et al. Clinical practice guideline: the diagnosis, management, and prevention of bronchiolitis. Pediatrics. 2014;134(5):e1474-e1502.
Kleinman ME, Chameides L, Schexnayder SM, et al. Part 14: Pediatric Advanced Life Support. Circulation. 2010;122(18 Suppl 3):S876-908.
Zorc JJ, Hall CB. Bronchiolitis: recent evidence on diagnosis and management. Pediatrics. 2010;125(2):342-349.
linked_flashcard_prompts: Retraction severity hierarchy subcostal to supraclavicular?
What does grunting indicate in an infant?
Inspiratory vs. biphasic stridor — what location?
Normal RR for newborn, infant, toddler, school-age, adolescent?
Why is a silent chest in asthma a pre-arrest emergency?
Why is SpO2 unreliable in CO poisoning?
Tripod/sniffing position — what does it mean?
Polyphonic vs. monophonic wheeze — what do they indicate?
Five signs of respiratory failure vs. distress?
What waveform finding makes SpO2 unreliable?

commonMistakes: Interpreting a quiet child with asthma as improving
Missing grunting as a failure sign
Trusting SpO2 in CO poisoning or poor perfusion
examMeta.examTips: REX-PN questions on respiratory assessment often test the distinction between distress and failure. Know that grunting = failure, silent chest = emergency, and that vital signs alone do not replace validated assessment. Stridor questions commonly ask about phase and location — link inspiratory to upper/laryngeal, biphasic to fixed obstruction.

ca-rpn-rex-pn.title: Cardiovascular Assessment in Children
ca-rpn-rex-pn.seoTitle: Cardiovascular Assessment in Children | REx-PN Pediatric Nursing | NurseNest
ca-rpn-rex-pn.seoDescription: REx-PN lesson: Pediatric cardiovascular assessment including heart sounds, murmur grading, capillary refill, central vs. peripheral cyanosis, pulse quality, hepatomegaly, and signs of heart failure in children.
ca-rpn-rex-pn.examBlueprintCompetency: Performs cardiovascular assessment in children including heart sounds, peripheral perfusion, and identifies abnormal findings
studyTakeaways: Central cyanosis (lips, tongue, mucous membranes, trunk) indicates arterial desaturation requiring cardiac or respiratory investigation; peripheral cyanosis (extremities only) is normal in newborns within the first 24–48 hours and in cold environments.

Capillary refill time (CRT): apply pressure for exactly 5 seconds, then count seconds to return; normal <2 seconds in extremities, <3 seconds centrally (forehead, sternum); unreliable when ambient temperature is cold.

Still's murmur (innocent murmur) features: grade 1–3, systolic, vibratory/musical quality, left lower sternal border, no radiation, changes with position, no thrill, no associated symptoms — if all present, no further workup typically needed.
Hepatomegaly (>2–3 cm below right costal margin) is a sign of right heart failure in children; combined with tachycardia and respiratory distress it suggests cardiac decompensation.
studyCommonTraps: Confusing acrocyanosis (normal peripheral cyanosis in newborns) with central cyanosis — acrocyanosis spares the lips and tongue and does not require intervention; central cyanosis always does.
Forgetting that capillary refill time is unreliable in cold ambient temperatures — always consider environmental temperature when interpreting CRT.
Grading murmurs incorrectly: a murmur with a palpable thrill is at minimum grade IV (grade I–III cannot have a thrill); this distinction is frequently tested.
ca-rpn-rex-pn.memoryAnchor: CRT: press 5 seconds, count return. Central cyanosis = lips + tongue = arterial problem. Peripheral only = acrocyanosis = normal in cold/newborn. Thrill = grade IV minimum.

sections.heading: Learning Objectives
sections.body:
By the end of this lesson, the RPN will be able to:

1. Identify the correct auscultation technique for S1 and S2 heart sounds and describe their significance.
2. Describe the physiological split of S2 and distinguish it from a fixed split, which suggests atrial septal defect.
3. Apply the six-level murmur grading scale and identify features of innocent versus pathological murmurs.
4. Perform and correctly interpret capillary refill time assessment in children.
5. Distinguish central cyanosis from peripheral cyanosis (acrocyanosis) by location and clinical significance.
6. Assess pulse quality and identify bounding, full, weak, and absent pulses and their clinical significance.
7. Identify hepatomegaly as a sign of right-sided heart failure in children.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.body:
Cardiovascular assessment in children requires knowledge of both normal pediatric cardiovascular physiology and the spectrum of findings that may indicate congenital or acquired heart disease. Congenital heart disease (CHD) affects approximately 8 in 1,000 live births and is the most common category of serious congenital anomaly. Many children with CHD are identified in the neonatal period through routine screening, but some defects present later in infancy or childhood with symptoms such as poor feeding, tachycardia, failure to thrive, cyanosis, or heart murmur.

The RPN's cardiovascular assessment is not diagnostic — the RPN does not interpret echocardiograms or catheterization data — but accurate assessment of perfusion, cardiac rhythm, heart sounds, murmur characteristics, and systemic effects of cardiac compromise (hepatomegaly, edema, poor feeding) is within the RPN scope and is critical for identifying children who need escalation.

This lesson covers the components of a systematic pediatric cardiovascular assessment, with emphasis on the physical examination findings most likely to appear on the REx-PN examination and most relevant to RPN practice in pediatric settings.

sections.heading: Core Content: Cardiovascular Physical Examination in Children
sections.body:
**Heart Sounds**

S1 (first heart sound): produced by closure of the mitral and tricuspid valves at the beginning of systole. Heard as 'lub.' Loudest at the apex (5th intercostal space, midclavicular line). Best heard with the diaphragm of the stethoscope.

S2 (second heart sound): produced by closure of the aortic (A2) and pulmonic (P2) valves at the beginning of diastole. Heard as 'dub.' Loudest at the base of the heart (left upper sternal border = pulmonic area; right upper sternal border = aortic area).

Physiological split of S2: Aortic valve closes slightly before pulmonic during expiration. On inspiration, decreased intrathoracic pressure increases venous return to the right heart, delaying pulmonic valve closure — the split between A2 and P2 widens. This physiological splitting on inspiration is normal. A fixed split (split that does not change with respiration) is associated with atrial septal defect (ASD), where the right heart fills equally regardless of respiratory phase.

S3 gallop: A low-pitched sound heard in early diastole, after S2. May be normal in children and young adults (physiological S3). Becomes pathological when associated with tachycardia and signs of heart failure — indicating rapid ventricular filling under high pressure. The combination of tachycardia + S3 + hepatomegaly + respiratory distress suggests cardiac decompensation.

**Heart Murmur Grading (Levine Scale)**

Murmurs are graded I through VI based on intensity:
- Grade I: barely audible even in a quiet room; requires careful listening
- Grade II: quiet but heard immediately upon placement of stethoscope
- Grade III: moderately loud; no associated palpable thrill
- Grade IV: loud; palpable thrill present
- Grade V: very loud; audible with stethoscope edge tilted off chest
- Grade VI: audible without stethoscope

Key rule: A PALPABLE THRILL = minimum Grade IV. A murmur cannot have a thrill and be Grade I, II, or III.

Murmur descriptors also include: timing (systolic, diastolic, continuous), location of maximum intensity, radiation (to axilla, back, neck), and quality (harsh, blowing, musical, vibratory).

**Innocent Murmurs (Still's Murmur)**

Still's murmur is the most common innocent murmur in children aged 2–6 years. Features: Grade I–III; systolic (never diastolic or continuous); vibratory, musical, or twanging quality; best heard at left lower sternal border or between the left lower sternal border and apex; changes with position (louder supine, softer or absent sitting/standing); no thrill; no radiation; no associated symptoms (no cyanosis, no exercise intolerance, no chest pain); completely normal cardiac anatomy.

Diastolic murmurs are NEVER innocent — they always require further investigation.

**Capillary Refill Time (CRT)**

Technique: Apply firm pressure to the nail bed (or skin on forehead or sternum for central CRT) for exactly 5 seconds. Release and count seconds until color returns. Normal: <2 seconds in extremities, <3 seconds centrally (forehead, sternum). Prolonged CRT indicates impaired perfusion (dehydration, shock, cardiac failure). Unreliable in cold ambient temperatures — environmental warmth must be ensured for valid interpretation.

**Central vs. Peripheral Cyanosis**

Central cyanosis: blue/dusky discoloration of the lips, tongue, mucous membranes, and trunk. Indicates arterial oxygen desaturation (typically SpO2 <85–90% when clinically visible). Causes: cyanotic congenital heart disease (right-to-left shunt), severe respiratory failure, methemoglobinemia. Always requires evaluation — never a normal finding after the immediate newborn period.

Peripheral cyanosis (acrocyanosis): blue discoloration of the hands and feet only. Lips and tongue remain pink. Does NOT indicate arterial desaturation. Caused by peripheral vasoconstriction and increased peripheral oxygen extraction. Normal in newborns for the first 24–48 hours of life and in children who are cold. No intervention required if central colour is pink and SpO2 is normal.

**Pulse Assessment**

In infants, the brachial pulse is preferred over the radial pulse for assessment. Central pulses (carotid, femoral, brachial in infants) are more reliable than peripheral pulses in low cardiac output states.

Pulse quality:
- Bounding: forceful, easily compressible; seen in hypervolemia, fever, anaemia, patent ductus arteriosus, aortic regurgitation
- Full/normal: easily palpable, normal volume
- Weak/thready: difficult to palpate, easily compressible; seen in poor cardiac output, severe dehydration, shock
- Absent: emergency requiring immediate action

**Hepatomegaly**

In young children and infants, the liver edge is palpable 1–2 cm below the right costal margin as a normal finding. Greater than 2–3 cm below the right costal margin constitutes hepatomegaly. In the context of heart disease, hepatomegaly is a sign of right ventricular failure and elevated venous pressure. Assess in right lower quadrant, moving fingers upward toward the right costal margin during inspiration.

sections.heading: Nursing Assessment: Systematic Pediatric Cardiovascular Examination
sections.body:
**Systematic approach to cardiovascular assessment in children:**

**Step 1 — General Observation**
Before touching the child, observe skin colour (pink, pale, mottled, cyanotic), breathing effort (tachypnea is a common early sign of heart failure in infants), nutritional status (failure to thrive can be a sign of CHD), activity level, and responsiveness.

**Step 2 — Vital Signs**
Heart rate by age (normal ranges to know): Newborn 100–160 bpm; Infant 100–150 bpm; Toddler 90–150 bpm; Preschool 80–140 bpm; School-age 70–120 bpm; Adolescent 60–100 bpm. Blood pressure: use appropriate cuff size (cuff width = 40% of arm circumference). Pulse pressure (systolic minus diastolic): wide pulse pressure = bounding pulse conditions (PDA, fever, aortic regurgitation); narrow pulse pressure = poor cardiac output.

**Step 3 — Skin and Perfusion**
Assess skin temperature (central warm vs. cool peripheries), colour, capillary refill time (technique as above), presence of mottling (irregular skin discoloration pattern indicating poor microcirculation).

**Step 4 — Auscultation**
Auscultate in a quiet room if possible. In children, the normal heart rate is faster, so distinguishing systole from diastole requires identifying S1 and S2 first. Listen at all five standard areas: aortic (right 2nd intercostal), pulmonic (left 2nd intercostal), Erb's point (left 3rd intercostal — best for aortic regurgitation), tricuspid (left 4th–5th intercostal), mitral/apex (5th intercostal, midclavicular line). Note S1, S2, any splitting of S2, presence of S3, and any additional sounds (murmur, click, rub).

**Step 5 — Pulse Assessment**
Palpate central and peripheral pulses. Note rate, rhythm (regular, irregular), and quality. Compare upper and lower extremity pulses — discrepancy (full brachial pulse but weak femoral) suggests coarctation of the aorta.

**Step 6 — Abdomen**
Palpate for hepatomegaly. Assess for splenomegaly (less common in cardiac disease). Note dependent edema (periorbital edema and facial puffiness are common presentations of fluid overload in infants rather than ankle edema).

**Step 7 — Respiratory**
In heart failure, respiratory compromise co-occurs — assess respiratory rate, work of breathing, and oxygen saturation as part of the cardiovascular assessment, as tachypnea and crackles indicate pulmonary congestion.

sections.heading: Nursing Interventions for Cardiovascular Assessment Findings
sections.body:
**For a child with cyanosis:**
Differentiate central from peripheral immediately. Central cyanosis (lips, tongue) = apply supplemental oxygen and report to RN immediately. Assess oxygen saturation with pulse oximetry (recognizing limitations). Position upright to reduce work of breathing. Prepare for urgent provider assessment.

**For a child with prolonged capillary refill (>3 seconds):**
Consider environmental temperature — warm the child and reassess. If ambient temperature is normal and CRT remains prolonged, this indicates impaired perfusion: report to RN, monitor vital signs closely, ensure IV access, and assess for shock signs (altered mental status, tachycardia, weak pulses).

**For a child with tachycardia:**
Assess for reversible causes first: fever, pain, anxiety, dehydration. If tachycardia persists beyond expected physiology or is associated with poor perfusion, respiratory distress, or altered mental status, report immediately. Document heart rate, rhythm, associated findings, and response to interventions.

**For a murmur noted on assessment:**
Document the murmur's location, timing, grade (if able), and associated findings. Report to the RN any new murmur or any murmur with associated cyanosis, exercise intolerance, altered perfusion, or failure to thrive. The RPN does not independently diagnose murmur etiology but documents and reports accurately.

**For a child with signs of heart failure (tachycardia + hepatomegaly + respiratory distress + poor feeding):**
Report immediately to the RN. Position upright, administer supplemental oxygen as ordered, restrict activity, and monitor intake and output carefully. Weigh daily for fluid retention. Prepare for provider assessment, ECG, and possible echocardiogram.

sections.heading: Parent and Family Teaching
sections.body:
**Teaching parents about cardiovascular signs in children focuses on recognition of warning signs and demystifying normal findings such as innocent murmurs.**

**Teaching about innocent murmurs:**
'Your child has a murmur that has been evaluated and found to be innocent — this means it is caused by normal blood flow through a normal heart. Innocent murmurs are very common in children and do not require treatment, do not limit activity, and do not increase the risk of heart problems. The murmur may come and go and may change with your child's position or activity level. You do not need to restrict your child from sports or physical activity.'

**Warning signs to report to a provider (may indicate cardiac compromise):**
- Skin or lips turning blue or grey
- Fast breathing or difficulty breathing, especially during feeding
- Infant who takes longer than 30 minutes to complete a feeding, sweats with feeding, or is too tired to finish feeding
- Poor weight gain or weight loss
- Unusual fatigue with activity (older child who cannot keep up with peers, stops playing due to shortness of breath or chest pain)
- Fainting or loss of consciousness during exercise
- Rapid or irregular heartbeat felt by the child
- Swelling of the face, hands, or feet that is new

**Teaching about acrocyanosis in newborns:**
'It is normal for newborn babies to have blue hands and feet in the first day or two of life. This is called acrocyanosis and happens because blood is concentrated in the baby's core organs. As long as your baby's lips and tongue are pink, this is normal. If the lips or tongue are blue, that needs immediate medical attention.'

sections.heading: Safety Considerations
sections.body:
**Safety considerations in pediatric cardiovascular assessment:**

**Coarctation of the aorta detection:** Compare upper and lower extremity pulses. Brachial pulses that are strong and femoral pulses that are weak or absent is a classic sign of coarctation of the aorta — a potentially life-threatening congenital defect. All newborns should have femoral pulse assessment as part of their examination. Report any discrepancy immediately.

**Newborn pulse oximetry screening:** Current Canadian guidelines recommend universal pulse oximetry screening for critical CHD in newborns at 24–48 hours of age or before discharge. Screen in the right hand (pre-ductal) and either foot (post-ductal). A difference of >3% between pre- and post-ductal saturation is a positive screen requiring further assessment.

**Prostaglandin E1 (PGE1) and ductal-dependent lesions:** Some neonates with cyanotic congenital heart disease are 'ductal-dependent' — the ductus arteriosus must remain patent to maintain pulmonary blood flow. These infants receive PGE1 infusion. Nursing responsibility: monitor for PGE1 side effects (apnea — most serious; fever; hypotension; cutaneous vasodilation). Have bag-valve-mask at bedside. Never discontinue PGE1 without provider order.

**Medication safety in cardiac children:** Digoxin is a high-alert cardiac medication used in pediatric heart failure. Calculate the dose carefully by weight (micrograms/kg). Check apical heart rate for 60 seconds before administration — hold if HR <60–70 bpm in school-age or adolescent, <80–90 bpm in infant (facility-specific parameters). Digoxin toxicity signs: nausea/vomiting, bradycardia, dysrhythmia, yellow-green visual halos.

sections.heading: Documentation
sections.body:
**Complete cardiovascular assessment documentation for the pediatric patient:**

1. **Heart rate and rhythm:** exact rate, auscultated for 60 seconds; rhythm (regular, regularly irregular, irregularly irregular).
2. **Blood pressure:** value, extremity used (right arm preferred), cuff size used.
3. **Heart sounds:** S1 and S2 quality (normal, muffled, loud, accentuated); S2 splitting (physiological, fixed); presence of S3 or S4; presence of clicks or rubs.
4. **Murmur:** if present — timing (systolic/diastolic/continuous), location of maximum intensity (use standard anatomical terms), grade (I–VI), radiation, quality (vibratory, harsh, blowing), change with position; whether thrill is palpable.
5. **Pulses:** central and peripheral pulse quality (bounding, full, weak, absent) and comparison of upper to lower extremity pulses.
6. **Capillary refill time:** exact time in seconds, location assessed (extremity/central), ambient temperature if cold.
7. **Colour:** pink/pale/mottled/cyanotic; if cyanosis — specify central (lips, tongue) versus peripheral (extremities only).
8. **Peripheral perfusion:** skin temperature (warm centrally/cool peripherally), presence of edema (location, degree).
9. **Hepatomegaly:** liver edge palpation findings in centimetres below right costal margin.
10. **Interventions and notifications:** any actions taken and RN or provider communication.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.body:
**Pearl 1:** A diastolic murmur is NEVER innocent. All diastolic murmurs in children require investigation and provider notification. Systolic murmurs may be innocent; diastolic ones are not.

**Pearl 2:** The palpable thrill = minimum grade IV. This is a frequently tested fact. A question that describes a murmur with a thrill that you are asked to grade — the answer cannot be I, II, or III.

**Pearl 3:** Acrocyanosis (hands and feet blue) in a newborn is normal for the first 24–48 hours. Central cyanosis (lips and tongue blue) is always abnormal and always requires assessment.

**Pearl 4:** Fixed split of S2 (does not change with respiration) is associated with atrial septal defect. On the exam, if a child has a murmur and you are told the split of S2 is fixed, ASD is the likely diagnosis.

**Pearl 5:** In infants with heart failure, the presentation differs from adults: poor feeding, diaphoresis with feeding, failure to thrive, tachypnea, and hepatomegaly — NOT ankle edema or chest pain.

**Pearl 6:** Pulse discrepancy (strong brachial, weak femoral) = coarctation of the aorta. This is a classic exam question. All newborns should have femoral pulse assessment.

**Pearl 7:** Still's murmur memory: VIBRATORY, SYSTOLIC, LEFT LOWER STERNAL BORDER, changes with POSITION, Grade I–III. These 5 features = innocent. If the murmur is loud (>III), diastolic, doesn't change with position, or has a thrill — refer.

**Pearl 8:** CRT technique: EXACTLY 5 seconds of pressure, then count. Rushing the compression time gives invalid results.

preTest.question: A newborn at 18 hours of life has blue hands and feet but pink lips and tongue. SpO2 on the right hand is 98%. What is the most appropriate nursing response?
options: A. Apply supplemental oxygen immediately for peripheral cyanosis
B. Notify the provider of suspected cyanotic congenital heart disease
C. Document findings as acrocyanosis — a normal finding in a newborn in the first 24–48 hours
D. Place the newborn under a radiant warmer to warm extremities before further assessment
preTest.rationale: Acrocyanosis (blue extremities only) with pink lips and tongue and normal SpO2 is a normal finding in newborns for the first 24–48 hours due to peripheral vasoconstriction and increased peripheral oxygen extraction. No intervention is required. Central cyanosis (blue lips/tongue) would require immediate action.

distractorRationales.A: Supplemental oxygen is not indicated for normal acrocyanosis; central colour and SpO2 are both normal.
distractorRationales.B: There is no indication of cyanotic CHD — central colour is pink and SpO2 is 98%.
distractorRationales.D: While warming extremities may resolve acrocyanosis, the first action is to document normal findings; placing under radiant warmer is not a clinical priority in this scenario.

preTest.question: When performing capillary refill time assessment in a child, which technique is correct?
options: A. Apply pressure for 2 seconds, then count until color returns
B. Apply pressure for 5 seconds, release, and count seconds until color returns
C. Observe the nail bed color without pressing and estimate refill
D. Apply pressure for 10 seconds to ensure the nail bed is fully blanched
preTest.rationale: Standardized CRT technique requires applying firm pressure for exactly 5 seconds, releasing, and counting seconds until color returns. Normal: <2 seconds in extremities, <3 seconds centrally. Deviating from the 5-second compression creates non-standardized and unreliable results.

distractorRationales.A: 2-second compression is not long enough to standardize blanching; results would be inconsistent and unreliable.
distractorRationales.C: Passive observation without compression does not assess capillary refill — active pressure is required to produce blanching.
distractorRationales.D: 10-second compression exceeds the standard and invalidates comparison to published normal values.

preTest.question: A 4-year-old is noted to have a grade II/VI systolic murmur at the left lower sternal border that has a vibratory quality, is softer when the child sits up, has no thrill, and the child has no symptoms. Which interpretation is most appropriate?
options: A. This murmur requires urgent cardiology referral due to its location
B. This is consistent with an innocent Still's murmur; document and notify the RN for physician assessment
C. The vibratory quality indicates turbulent flow and the murmur must be treated
D. A grade II murmur cannot be innocent; grade I is the only innocent grade
preTest.rationale: This murmur has all features of Still's murmur (most common innocent murmur): grade I–III, systolic, vibratory quality, left lower sternal border, changes with position (softer sitting up), no thrill, no symptoms. The RPN documents and reports but recognizes this is the classic innocent murmur presentation.

distractorRationales.A: Urgent cardiology referral is not indicated for a murmur with all features of Still's murmur; routine physician assessment is appropriate.
distractorRationales.C: Vibratory quality is characteristic of innocent (Still's) murmur — it does not indicate pathological turbulence.
distractorRationales.D: Innocent murmurs include grades I–III; grade II is very commonly innocent in children.

preTest.question: During assessment of a 3-day-old newborn, the nurse finds strong brachial pulses but cannot palpate femoral pulses. This finding suggests:
options: A. Normal finding; femoral pulses are not reliably palpable in newborns
B. Possible coarctation of the aorta requiring immediate reporting
C. Patent ductus arteriosus causing bounding pulses in all extremities
D. Dehydration causing peripheral pulse weakening
preTest.rationale: Absent or diminished femoral pulses compared to brachial pulses in a newborn is a classic sign of coarctation of the aorta, where the narrowed descending aorta limits blood flow to the lower body. This is a potentially life-threatening congenital defect requiring immediate provider notification.

distractorRationales.A: Femoral pulses should be palpable in normal newborns; absent femoral pulses are never a normal finding.
distractorRationales.C: PDA typically causes bounding pulses in all extremities, not absent femoral pulses with strong brachial pulses.
distractorRationales.D: Dehydration causes generalized pulse weakening; isolated absent femoral pulses with strong brachial pulses points specifically to coarctation.

preTest.question: Which murmur characteristic requires immediate reporting to the RN regardless of other findings?
options: A. Grade II systolic murmur at left lower sternal border
B. Murmur that changes with position
C. Diastolic murmur of any grade
D. Musical quality without associated symptoms
preTest.rationale: Diastolic murmurs are NEVER innocent in children and always require medical evaluation. Unlike systolic murmurs (which include many innocent variants), diastolic murmurs always indicate structural heart disease. Any diastolic murmur must be reported immediately.

distractorRationales.A: A grade II systolic murmur at the left lower sternal border with innocent features (vibratory, changes with position, no thrill) is commonly innocent and requires documentation and routine reporting, not immediate escalation.
distractorRationales.B: Murmurs that change with position are a reassuring feature of innocent murmurs.
distractorRationales.D: Musical quality is characteristic of the innocent Still's murmur and is not a concerning feature when present without other abnormal signs.

preTest.question: A 6-month-old infant is brought to the clinic because of poor weight gain and fatigue with feeding. On assessment the nurse finds a respiratory rate of 62/minute, hepatomegaly, and a heart rate of 168 bpm. What condition do these findings most suggest?
options: A. Normal findings for a 6-month-old during feeding stress
B. Dehydration causing tachycardia and hepatomegaly
C. Congestive heart failure in infancy presenting with failure to thrive, tachypnea, tachycardia, and hepatomegaly
D. Sepsis causing multiorgan stress
preTest.rationale: In infants, heart failure (CHF) presents differently than in adults. Classic infant CHF signs include: poor feeding/failure to thrive, tachypnea, tachycardia, diaphoresis with feeding, and hepatomegaly (right ventricular failure sign). The combination of all these findings in a 6-month-old with poor weight gain is classic for CHF until proven otherwise.

distractorRationales.A: RR 62 and HR 168 are above normal for a 6-month-old (normal RR 25–50, normal HR 100–150) even with feeding stress; hepatomegaly is never a normal finding.
distractorRationales.B: Dehydration does not cause hepatomegaly; it causes decreased skin turgor, concentrated urine, and sunken fontanel.
distractorRationales.D: While sepsis causes tachycardia and tachypnea, hepatomegaly is not a typical early sepsis finding; the combination with feeding-related failure to thrive better suggests cardiac etiology.

preTest.question: What is the clinical significance of a fixed split of S2?
options: A. Normal finding associated with deep inspiration
B. Sign of physiological respiratory variation in healthy children
C. Associated with atrial septal defect — requires investigation
D. Indicates poor cardiac contractility
preTest.rationale: A fixed split of S2 (split that does not vary with respiration) is associated with atrial septal defect (ASD). In ASD, the right heart fills at a constant rate regardless of respiratory phase because of the left-to-right shunt, eliminating the normal respiratory variation in pulmonic valve closure timing.

distractorRationales.A: Deep inspiration widens the physiological S2 split normally — this is NOT a fixed split.
distractorRationales.B: Physiological S2 splitting changes with respiration (wider on inspiration) — fixed splitting does not change with respiration and is abnormal.
distractorRationales.D: Fixed split S2 is a valvular/septal finding, not directly indicative of poor contractility.

preTest.question: The nurse auscultates a grade IV murmur. Which of the following must be present at grade IV?
options: A. The murmur is audible without a stethoscope
B. A palpable thrill accompanies the murmur
C. The murmur radiates to the axilla
D. The murmur is continuous throughout systole and diastole
preTest.rationale: By the Levine grading scale, a grade IV murmur is defined as loud with a palpable thrill. The palpable thrill (vibration felt with the hand) is the defining feature that distinguishes grades I–III (no thrill) from grades IV–VI (thrill present). Audibility without a stethoscope defines grade VI.

distractorRationales.A: Audibility without a stethoscope defines grade VI, not grade IV.
distractorRationales.C: Radiation is a descriptor of murmur quality and is not part of the grading scale.
distractorRationales.D: Continuous murmurs (systolic and diastolic) describe specific pathology (PDA) but are not part of the grade definition.

preTest.question: In an infant with poor cardiac output (cardiogenic shock), which pulse finding would the nurse expect?
options: A. Bounding pulses bilaterally
B. Wide pulse pressure
C. Weak, thready central and peripheral pulses
D. Strong femoral pulses with absent brachial pulses
preTest.rationale: Poor cardiac output reduces stroke volume, producing weak and thready pulses. In cardiogenic shock, the failing heart cannot generate adequate forward flow, resulting in diminished pulse amplitude. Bounding pulses indicate high output states (fever, PDA, anaemia). Absent femoral with strong brachial suggests coarctation.

distractorRationales.A: Bounding pulses indicate hypervolemia or high cardiac output states — the opposite of cardiogenic shock.
distractorRationales.B: Wide pulse pressure is associated with bounding pulse conditions (aortic regurgitation, PDA, fever); narrow pulse pressure occurs in cardiogenic shock.
distractorRationales.D: Absent femoral with strong brachial is the pattern of coarctation of the aorta, not cardiogenic shock.

preTest.question: When auscultating for the S1 heart sound, where is it heard most clearly?
options: A. Left upper sternal border (pulmonic area)
B. Right upper sternal border (aortic area)
C. Apex — 5th intercostal space at midclavicular line
D. Erb's point — left 3rd intercostal space
preTest.rationale: S1 is produced by closure of the mitral and tricuspid valves at the beginning of systole. It is heard loudest at the apex (mitral area): 5th intercostal space at the midclavicular line. S2 is heard loudest at the base (pulmonic and aortic areas).

distractorRationales.A: The left upper sternal border is the pulmonic area — best for hearing pulmonic component of S2 and pulmonic valve pathology.
distractorRationales.B: The right upper sternal border is the aortic area — best for hearing aortic component of S2.
distractorRationales.D: Erb's point (left 3rd intercostal space) is best for hearing aortic regurgitation and some murmur radiation, not S1 specifically.

postTest.question: A 5-year-old child has a CRT of 4 seconds in the right hand. The emergency room ambient temperature is 18°C (cold). What should the nurse do first?
options: A. Report immediately to the RN as a sign of shock
B. Warm the child's hand and reassess CRT before reporting findings
C. Document CRT of 4 seconds and continue routine assessment
D. Administer an IV fluid bolus per protocol for prolonged CRT
postTest.rationale: CRT is unreliable in cold ambient temperatures — peripheral vasoconstriction from cold extends CRT without indicating impaired perfusion. The correct first action is to warm the extremity and reassess before attributing the finding to a pathological cause. If CRT remains prolonged after warming, then report to the RN.

distractorRationales.A: Reporting immediately without addressing the known confounder (cold temperature) risks over-reporting a potentially normal finding; confirm by warming first.
distractorRationales.C: Documenting and moving on ignores a potentially significant finding — reassessment after warming is required.
distractorRationales.D: Administering IV fluid without confirming pathological CRT is premature and potentially unsafe.

postTest.question: A 2-year-old with a known ventricular septal defect (VSD) presents with worsening respiratory distress and a heart rate of 180 bpm. On assessment, the liver edge is 4 cm below the right costal margin. What does the hepatomegaly most likely indicate?
options: A. Normal liver position in a 2-year-old
B. Viral hepatitis complicating the VSD
C. Right ventricular failure with elevated venous pressure causing hepatic engorgement
D. Dehydration causing relative hepatomegaly
postTest.rationale: Hepatomegaly of 4 cm below the right costal margin (normal is ≤2 cm in toddlers) in the context of tachycardia and respiratory distress in a child with CHD indicates right heart failure. Elevated right-sided venous pressure causes hepatic venous congestion and hepatomegaly.

distractorRationales.A: 1–2 cm below the right costal margin is normal in toddlers; 4 cm constitutes hepatomegaly.
distractorRationales.B: While hepatitis can cause hepatomegaly, the clinical context (CHD, tachycardia, respiratory distress) points strongly to cardiac failure.
distractorRationales.D: Dehydration does not cause hepatomegaly; it reduces blood volume and would not engorge the liver.

postTest.question: The nurse is caring for a neonate receiving prostaglandin E1 (PGE1) for suspected ductal-dependent congenital heart disease. The nurse notes apnea. What is the most appropriate action?
options: A. Increase the PGE1 infusion rate to maintain ductal patency
B. Discontinue PGE1 immediately and report to the provider
C. Stimulate the neonate gently, apply supplemental oxygen, and be prepared to provide bag-valve-mask ventilation; report to the RN immediately
D. Document apnea as an expected finding and continue monitoring
postTest.rationale: Apnea is the most serious adverse effect of PGE1. The nurse should stimulate the neonate, be prepared for bag-valve-mask ventilation if stimulation is insufficient, apply supplemental oxygen, and report immediately to the RN. PGE1 should not be discontinued without provider order, as cessation can cause the ductus to close, which may be life-threatening in a ductal-dependent lesion.

distractorRationales.A: Increasing PGE1 is not the response to apnea; the apnea is caused by PGE1 and must be managed while the drug is assessed.
distractorRationales.B: Never discontinue PGE1 without a provider order — the ductus may close, worsening cyanosis acutely.
distractorRationales.D: Apnea is a significant adverse effect requiring active intervention, not passive documentation.

postTest.question: Newborn pulse oximetry screening involves measuring SpO2 in which two locations?
options: A. Both feet (bilateral post-ductal)
B. Right hand (pre-ductal) and either foot (post-ductal)
C. Left hand (post-ductal) and right hand (pre-ductal)
D. Both hands (bilateral pre-ductal)
postTest.rationale: Universal newborn pulse oximetry screening for critical CHD measures SpO2 in the right hand (pre-ductal — reflects oxygen content before the ductus arteriosus) and either foot (post-ductal — reflects oxygen after the ductus). A difference >3% between pre- and post-ductal readings is a positive screen.

distractorRationales.A: Bilateral feet would give two post-ductal readings; you need one pre-ductal (right hand) for comparison.
distractorRationales.C: The left hand is post-ductal (subclavian artery arises after the ductus in anatomy); right hand is pre-ductal.
distractorRationales.D: Both hands would not detect a ductal-dependent lesion with difference between pre and post-ductal sites.

postTest.question: Which clinical finding most strongly suggests the child's murmur is NOT innocent?
options: A. Grade II/VI intensity
B. Left lower sternal border location
C. Vibratory quality
D. Diastolic timing
postTest.rationale: Diastolic murmurs are never innocent — they always indicate structural heart pathology and require immediate investigation. Systolic murmurs may be innocent; diastolic ones cannot be. Grade II, left lower sternal border location, and vibratory quality are all features consistent with innocent Still's murmur.

distractorRationales.A: Grade II is fully consistent with an innocent murmur (innocent murmurs are grades I–III).
distractorRationales.B: Left lower sternal border is the classic location of Still's murmur (most common innocent murmur).
distractorRationales.C: Vibratory/musical quality is a defining characteristic of the innocent Still's murmur.

postTest.question: The nurse is assessing a 3-year-old with a heart rate of 160 bpm. The child has a fever of 39.2°C. Before reporting tachycardia to the provider, what should the nurse do?
options: A. Report immediately as HR 160 is above normal for a 3-year-old
B. Administer antipyretic as ordered and reassess heart rate after fever is reduced
C. Order an ECG to rule out dysrhythmia before giving antipyretic
D. Document as normal, as children with fever always have elevated heart rates
postTest.rationale: Fever is a common reversible cause of sinus tachycardia. For a 3-year-old (normal HR 90–150), a HR of 160 during a fever of 39.2°C is likely fever-driven. Administer antipyretic as ordered and reassess — if tachycardia persists after fever reduction or if there are signs of poor perfusion, escalate. Treating the reversible cause first is appropriate nursing assessment.

distractorRationales.A: Reporting without addressing the obvious reversible cause (fever) is premature and may lead to unnecessary workup.
distractorRationales.C: Ordering an ECG is outside the RPN scope of independent action; the nurse can request but cannot order diagnostic tests.
distractorRationales.D: Tachycardia with fever still requires documentation and follow-up — it is not simply 'expected and normal' without further assessment.

postTest.question: S3 heart sound in an infant with heart failure most likely represents:
options: A. Normal finding — S3 is always physiological in infants
B. Rapid ventricular filling under elevated filling pressure, indicating heart failure when combined with tachycardia
C. Closure of the tricuspid valve at the beginning of systole
D. Aortic stenosis causing turbulence in early diastole
postTest.rationale: S3 is a low-pitched early diastolic sound caused by rapid ventricular filling. In infants and young children, a physiological S3 may be normal. However, when S3 occurs with tachycardia and signs of heart failure (tachypnea, hepatomegaly, poor feeding), it is pathological and indicates elevated ventricular filling pressure — a hallmark of heart failure.

distractorRationales.A: S3 may be physiological in healthy children but is NOT always normal — context matters; with tachycardia and other heart failure signs it is pathological.
distractorRationales.C: Tricuspid valve closure at the beginning of systole produces S1, not S3.
distractorRationales.D: Aortic stenosis produces a systolic ejection murmur, not an S3 gallop.

postTest.question: A 10-year-old reports palpitations and dizziness during soccer practice. Which cardiovascular finding during the resting assessment would most concern the nurse?
options: A. Heart rate of 88 bpm regular rhythm
B. A grade I/VI systolic murmur at left sternal border without thrill
C. Prolonged CRT of 3 seconds on right hand after activity
D. Irregular heart rhythm at rest with occasional dropped beats
postTest.rationale: An irregular rhythm with dropped beats at rest is an abnormal finding that may represent a dysrhythmia. Combined with exercise-induced symptoms (palpitations, dizziness), this requires immediate reporting and ECG assessment. The other findings are benign: HR 88 is normal, grade I murmur is likely innocent, and CRT of 3 seconds immediately post-exercise is expected as peripheral blood is shunted.

distractorRationales.A: Heart rate of 88 bpm with regular rhythm in a resting 10-year-old is normal.
distractorRationales.B: Grade I systolic murmur without thrill at left sternal border is consistent with an innocent murmur.
distractorRationales.C: CRT may be mildly prolonged immediately post-exercise due to physiological redistribution; this alone is not alarming.

postTest.question: Which finding on cardiovascular assessment is most likely to indicate poor cardiac output rather than dehydration?
options: A. Dry mucous membranes and decreased urine output
B. Tachycardia with cool extremities, weak central pulses, and hepatomegaly
C. Sunken anterior fontanel and poor skin turgor in an infant
D. Orthostatic hypotension in an adolescent after vomiting
postTest.rationale: Tachycardia with cool extremities, weak central pulses, and hepatomegaly is the constellation of findings indicating poor cardiac output (cardiogenic etiology) rather than dehydration. Hepatomegaly specifically points to venous congestion from right heart failure. Dry mucous membranes, sunken fontanel, poor skin turgor, and orthostatic hypotension all indicate dehydration.

distractorRationales.A: Dry mucous membranes and decreased urine output indicate dehydration, not cardiac failure.
distractorRationales.C: Sunken fontanel and poor skin turgor are classic dehydration findings in infants.
distractorRationales.D: Orthostatic hypotension after vomiting in an adolescent indicates volume depletion (dehydration), not cardiac failure.

postTest.question: The nurse is teaching parents about their child's newly diagnosed innocent murmur. Which statement should be included in the teaching?
options: A. 'Your child should avoid contact sports and vigorous exercise until cleared by a cardiologist.'
B. 'The murmur will disappear permanently by age 12.'
C. 'An innocent murmur does not indicate any structural heart problem and does not limit your child's activity.'
D. 'You should have your child's murmur rechecked every 3 months.'
postTest.rationale: Innocent murmurs do not represent structural heart disease, do not require activity restriction, do not require routine echocardiography, and do not require frequent follow-up beyond normal well-child care. The most important teaching point is that the murmur does not indicate heart disease and does not limit activity.

distractorRationales.A: Activity restriction is not indicated for innocent murmurs; this teaching would cause unnecessary anxiety and harm quality of life.
distractorRationales.B: Many innocent murmurs persist into adulthood or may come and go; 'will permanently disappear by age 12' is incorrect and should not be taught.
distractorRationales.D: Quarterly murmur checks are not indicated for innocent murmurs — normal well-child care is sufficient.

flashcards.front: What are S1 and S2, when do they occur, and where are they heard loudest?
flashcards.back: S1 ('lub'): closure of mitral and tricuspid valves at beginning of systole. Heard loudest at APEX (5th ICS, midclavicular line). S2 ('dub'): closure of aortic and pulmonic valves at beginning of diastole. Heard loudest at BASE (left upper sternal border = pulmonic; right upper sternal border = aortic).
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Count heart rate using S1 as your reference point — S1 is the first sound in systole ('lub-DUB' = S1-S2). Apex for S1, base for S2.
flashcards.memoryAnchor: S1 = start of systole = apex. S2 = start of diastole = base. Apex-Base = S1-S2.

flashcards.front: What distinguishes a physiological S2 split from a fixed split, and what does a fixed split indicate?
flashcards.back: Physiological split: A2 closes before P2; split WIDENS on inspiration (increased venous return delays pulmonic closure). NORMAL. Fixed split: S2 split does NOT change with respiration — stays the same in both phases. Associated with ATRIAL SEPTAL DEFECT (ASD) where right heart fills at constant rate regardless of respiratory phase.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Fixed split = ASD on exam. If you hear a fixed S2 split with a systolic murmur at the left upper sternal border, ASD is the likely diagnosis.
flashcards.memoryAnchor: Physiological split breathes with you (widens with in-breath). Fixed split is STUCK — like an ASD-stuck shunt.

flashcards.front: What are the features of an innocent Still's murmur?
flashcards.back: Grade I–III (no thrill). SYSTOLIC only (never diastolic). VIBRATORY/musical/twanging quality. Location: LEFT LOWER STERNAL BORDER or between LLSB and apex. Changes with POSITION (louder supine, softer or absent sitting/standing). No radiation. No associated symptoms. Normal cardiac anatomy.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: A diastolic murmur is NEVER innocent. A murmur with a thrill is minimum grade IV — never innocent. Any murmur with cyanosis, exercise intolerance, or thrill must be investigated.
flashcards.memoryAnchor: STILL'S = Systolic, vibraTory, Innocent, Left Lower, Lies down louder, Symptoms absent.

flashcards.front: What is the Levine murmur grading scale? What is the minimum grade if a thrill is palpable?
flashcards.back: Grade I: barely audible in quiet room. Grade II: quiet but heard immediately. Grade III: moderately loud, no thrill. Grade IV: loud with PALPABLE THRILL. Grade V: audible with stethoscope tilted. Grade VI: audible without stethoscope. Palpable thrill = minimum GRADE IV. Cannot be grade I, II, or III with a thrill.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: The palpable thrill question is a classic test trap: 'A nurse feels a thrill — what is the minimum grade?' Answer: Grade IV.
flashcards.memoryAnchor: Thrill = Four and above. 'IV and up feel the throb.'

flashcards.front: How do you perform capillary refill time and what is the normal finding?
flashcards.back: Apply FIRM PRESSURE to nail bed for EXACTLY 5 SECONDS. Release. Count seconds until color returns. Normal: <2 seconds in extremities; <3 seconds centrally (forehead, sternum). Prolonged CRT = impaired perfusion. UNRELIABLE in cold ambient temperature — warm the child first and reassess.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Always note ambient temperature in your documentation when CRT is prolonged. A cold exam room is a CRT confounder that may generate unnecessary investigations.
flashcards.memoryAnchor: 5 seconds press, count to 2. 'Press 5, count 2, warm first.'

flashcards.front: What is the difference between central cyanosis and peripheral cyanosis (acrocyanosis)?
flashcards.back: Central cyanosis: blue/dusky lips, tongue, mucous membranes, trunk. Indicates ARTERIAL DESATURATION (<85–90% SpO2 when visible). Always abnormal after the immediate newborn period. Requires immediate assessment. Peripheral cyanosis (acrocyanosis): blue extremities (hands/feet) only; lips and tongue PINK. Does NOT indicate desaturation. Normal in newborns 0–48 hours. Normal in cold environment.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Exam tip: if the question says 'lips and tongue are pink' with blue hands, this is acrocyanosis — normal. If lips/tongue are blue, that is central cyanosis — emergency.
flashcards.memoryAnchor: Central = Core is blue = arterial problem. Peripheral = Fingers and toes only = vasoconstriction = normal variant.

flashcards.front: What pulse discrepancy suggests coarctation of the aorta in a newborn?
flashcards.back: Strong brachial pulses with absent or weak femoral pulses. Coarctation of the aorta narrows the descending aorta (typically just distal to the left subclavian artery), restricting blood flow to the lower body. Upper extremity BP is elevated; lower extremity BP is reduced. All newborns should have femoral pulse assessment as part of standard examination.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: Coarctation is frequently missed in newborns who are ductus-dependent — the ductus maintains lower body perfusion temporarily. Once the ductus closes (day 2–4 of life), coarctation may present with shock.
flashcards.memoryAnchor: Coarc = strong arms, weak legs. 'Coarctation coarcts (squeezes) the bottom half.'

flashcards.front: What are bounding pulses and what conditions cause them in children?
flashcards.back: Bounding pulses: forceful, easily palpable, feel like a strong wave against your fingertips; easily compressible. Causes: patent ductus arteriosus (PDA) — blood runs off into pulmonary circulation during diastole creating wide pulse pressure; fever; anaemia (high cardiac output); aortic regurgitation; hypervolemia. Associated with WIDE pulse pressure (high systolic, low diastolic).
flashcards.clinicalPearl: In a neonate with bounding pulses and a continuous machinery murmur under the left clavicle, think PDA. The bounding quality is from diastolic runoff into the pulmonary circulation.
flashcards.memoryAnchor: Bounding = blood running away (wide pulse pressure). PDA, fever, anemia = 'too much going, too fast.'

flashcards.front: What are the signs of heart failure in infants and how do they differ from adult heart failure?
flashcards.back: Infant CHF signs: poor feeding or feeding fatigue (takes >30 min, stops frequently), diaphoresis with feeding, failure to thrive, tachycardia, tachypnea, hepatomegaly, PERIORBITAL edema (not ankle edema). NOT: chest pain, ankle edema, or dyspnea on exertion as chief complaint. Infants cannot report symptoms — the nurse observes feeding behaviour, weight gain trend, and physical signs.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: A baby who sweats profusely during feeding and takes 45 minutes to finish half a bottle, with a respiratory rate of 65 and hepatomegaly, has heart failure until proven otherwise.
flashcards.memoryAnchor: Infant CHF = SWIFT: Sweating with feeds, Weight gain poor, Increased HR/RR, Feeding fatigue, Tender (hepatomegaly).

flashcards.front: What are the most serious adverse effects of prostaglandin E1 (PGE1) and what nursing action addresses the most dangerous one?
flashcards.back: PGE1 adverse effects: APNEA (most serious — can occur unpredictably, especially at higher doses), fever, hypotension, cutaneous vasodilation (flushing). Nursing action for apnea: stimulate the infant gently; if apnea persists, provide bag-valve-mask ventilation; DO NOT discontinue PGE1 without provider order (ductus closure can precipitate cyanotic crisis). Have resuscitation equipment at bedside at all times.
flashcards.clinicalPearl: All neonates on PGE1 should have bag-valve-mask and suction at the bedside; apnea can occur without warning. The nurse must be prepared to ventilate while maintaining the infusion.
flashcards.memoryAnchor: PGE1 = Potentially Gives Apnea. Keep the airway bag at the bedside, and don't stop the drip without an order.

imagePlaceholders.alt: Diagram of pediatric chest showing five standard cardiac auscultation areas with anatomical landmarks
imagePlaceholders.caption: Standard Cardiac Auscultation Areas in Children: Five auscultation sites with anatomical landmarks and best sounds heard at each location.
imagePlaceholders.suggestedContent: Anterior chest with labeled stethoscope positions: Aortic area (right 2nd ICS) — best for aortic valve sounds; Pulmonic area (left 2nd ICS) — best for pulmonic valve, S2 splitting; Erb's point (left 3rd ICS) — aortic regurgitation; Tricuspid area (left 4th-5th ICS sternal border); Mitral/Apex (5th ICS midclavicular line) — best for S1 and mitral sounds. Include rib numbering and clavicle landmarks.

imagePlaceholders.alt: Comparison diagram of central cyanosis versus peripheral cyanosis showing affected body areas
imagePlaceholders.caption: Central vs. Peripheral Cyanosis: Location of discoloration distinguishes arterial desaturation (central) from normal peripheral vasoconstriction (acrocyanosis).
imagePlaceholders.suggestedContent: Two infant body outlines side by side. Left (central cyanosis): blue shading over lips, tongue, trunk, and extremities — labeled 'arterial desaturation, always abnormal, requires assessment.' Right (peripheral cyanosis/acrocyanosis): blue shading on hands and feet only, pink trunk and lips — labeled 'normal in newborns 0-48h, normal in cold environment, no intervention required if SpO2 normal.'

documentationConsiderations: Document murmur with all descriptors: timing (systolic/diastolic/continuous), grade (I–VI using Levine scale), location of maximum intensity by anatomical area, radiation, quality (vibratory/harsh/blowing), and whether a thrill is palpable.
When documenting cyanosis, specify whether it is central (lips, tongue, mucous membranes) or peripheral (extremities only) — the distinction is clinically critical and determines urgency of response.
Document capillary refill time with exact seconds, the site tested, and ambient temperature if cold, as cold temperature is a documented CRT confounder.
For pulse assessment, document both central and peripheral pulse quality and explicitly compare upper to lower extremity pulses, as discrepancy may indicate coarctation of the aorta.
references: Hoffman JI, Kaplan S. The incidence of congenital heart disease. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2002;39(12):1890-1900.
Ewer AK, Middleton LJ, Furmston AT, et al. Pulse oximetry screening for congenital heart defects in newborn infants (PulseOx): a test accuracy study. Lancet. 2011;378(9793):785-794.
Pelech AN. The physiology of cardiac auscultation. Pediatr Clin North Am. 2004;51(6):1515-1535.
linked_flashcard_prompts: Where are S1 and S2 heard loudest?
What is a fixed S2 split and what does it indicate?
Features of an innocent Still's murmur?
Levine murmur grading — minimum grade with thrill?
How to perform CRT and what is normal?
Central vs. peripheral cyanosis — how to distinguish?
Pulse discrepancy suggesting coarctation of the aorta?
What causes bounding pulses in children?
Signs of heart failure in infants vs. adults?
PGE1 most serious adverse effect and nursing action?

commonMistakes: Stating grade I–III with palpable thrill
Missing hepatomegaly as a heart failure sign in infants
examMeta.examTips: REX-PN cardiovascular questions frequently test the innocent vs. pathological murmur distinction and the central vs. peripheral cyanosis distinction. Memorize: diastolic = never innocent, thrill = grade IV minimum, acrocyanosis = normal in newborn. Coarctation questions typically present with absent femoral pulses in a newborn.

ca-rpn-rex-pn.title: Neurological Assessment in Children
ca-rpn-rex-pn.seoTitle: Neurological Assessment in Children | REx-PN Pediatric Nursing | NurseNest
ca-rpn-rex-pn.seoDescription: REx-PN lesson: Pediatric neurological assessment including modified GCS, AVPU, pupillary responses, primitive reflexes, DTRs, meningeal signs, Cushing's triad, and sunset sign in hydrocephalus.
ca-rpn-rex-pn.examBlueprintCompetency: Performs neurological assessment adapted to pediatric age and identifies signs of neurological dysfunction and increased ICP
studyTakeaways: GCS <8 in a child indicates severe neurological impairment and is generally the threshold for intubation; GCS 9–12 = moderate; GCS 13–15 = mild; adapt verbal scoring for pre-verbal children (age-appropriate cooing/words = 5).

Cushing's Triad (hypertension + bradycardia + irregular/slow respirations) is a LATE and ominous sign of severely increased intracranial pressure and impending herniation — it demands immediate emergency response.
Babinski reflex (plantar extension with toe fanning) is NORMAL until age 2 years; it becomes a pathological sign of upper motor neuron (UMN) lesion only after age 2.
Meningeal signs (Kernig's, Brudzinski's, neck stiffness) may be absent in infants under 12 months with meningitis — fontanel bulging is a more reliable sign of increased ICP in this age group.
studyCommonTraps: Interpreting a normal Babinski response in a 10-month-old as pathological — plantar extension with toe fanning is NORMAL until age 2 and indicates intact corticospinal tract myelination is still in progress.
Assuming Cushing's Triad is an early warning sign — it is a LATE sign indicating the brain is herniating; by the time Cushing's appears, emergency intervention is immediately needed.
Missing meningitis in an infant because meningeal signs are absent — infants under 12 months may have negative Kernig's and Brudzinski's despite severe meningitis; fontanel assessment is essential.
